items.myth: Code review's job is to catch bugs.
items.reality: Bug-catching is *one* benefit. The bigger value is knowledge sharing, design discussion, training the team on conventions, and creating an audit trail. Bugs are mostly caught by tests; review catches design issues.
items.example: Review often surfaces 'this would be simpler with the existing helper' — that's worth more than catching one off-by-one.

items.reality: Above 1–2 reviewers per PR, marginal value drops fast while latency grows linearly. Two engaged reviewers catch more than five distracted ones.
items.example: Many top-tier teams require exactly 1 approval for small changes, 2 only for high-risk paths (security, payments).

items.myth: Approving a PR makes you co-responsible if it breaks.
items.reality: Approval signals 'I've read this and think it's safe to land'. Responsibility for the change still primarily sits with the author. (Some companies do count approvers in incident retros — but as a learning signal, not punishment.)
items.example: An approver who genuinely missed something is *information*, not a culprit — review the review process, not the person.

items.reality: Draft PRs run CI by default on most platforms; the 'draft' status just suppresses automatic review requests. Use drafts liberally to get early CI feedback without pulling reviewers in.
items.example: Open as draft → push frequently → flip to 'ready for review' only when CI is green and the diff is polished.

items.myth: Branch protection is paranoid for small teams.
items.reality: Branch protection is *especially* valuable for small teams — there are fewer humans to catch mistakes. The 'require PR + 1 review' rule costs nearly nothing and prevents the 'I pushed directly at 1am' class of incident.
items.example: Even a solo dev benefits: 'require PR' forces a self-review of the diff before merge.
